summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Steve Ballmer's infamous on-stage antics, presenting three classic videos as essential viewing for Windows developers. The post highlights Ballmer's willingness to embarrass himself publicly despite being a billionaire, from screaming until he needed vocal cord surgery to popping out of anniversary cakes. Atwood contrasts Ballmer's manic energy with the boring corporate style of competitors like Michael Dell. The post also briefly notes Ballmer's controversial positions on Linux, GPL, and DRM, but frames even these as part of his unique character. Ultimately, Atwood argues that Ballmer's sweaty enthusiasm is an asset for Microsoft's image compared to Linux's mascot.
key_points: Three Ballmer videos (Windows 1.0 pitch, Dance Monkeyboy, Developers chant) are presented as essential Windows developer viewing
Ballmer's willingness to make a fool of himself publicly is presented as admirable authenticity that most executives lack
His enthusiasm was so extreme he once required vocal cord surgery after screaming 'Windows' continuously
Ballmer held controversial views calling Linux 'communism' and the GPL 'a cancer' while advocating for DRM
The post playfully argues Ballmer is a better mascot for Microsoft than Tux is for Linux
money_quotes: I love the fact that Steve Ballmer is a billionaire 12 times over, and yet he isn't afraid to get up on stage and make a complete and utter ass out of himself.
You have to respect a man with that much unbridled enthusiasm.
I certainly can't see staid, boring old Michael Dell dancing around on stage, screaming about how much he loves his company at the top of his lungs.
His vocal cords required surgery after he screamed 'Windows, Windows, Windows' continuously at a Japan meeting in 1991.
A sweat-soaked Ballmer repeatedly chanted 'developers' at least 14 times in front of the bemused gathering.
Linux may have Tux, but we've got Ballmer. Pudgy cartoon penguin versus sweaty screaming billionaire? C'mon. That's not even close.
key_insight: Authentic enthusiasm and willingness to look foolish can be more compelling than polished corporate presentations, even—or especially—at the highest levels of business.
